current_fact.fact_text: In 1815, Prince Willem of Orange proclaimed himself King of the United Kingdom of the Netherlands, becoming the country’s first constitutional monarch. His rule signaled a shift away from absolute power toward a modern system where royal authority was bound by a written constitution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

current_fact.match_reason: The birth of a constitutional monarchy balancing power and restraint mirrors the episode’s play between fog and fire—two contrasting energies held within one political body.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 445, Western Roman Emperor Valentinian III was assassinated on the Campus Martius in Rome, reportedly struck down by soldiers avenging a slain general. His death accelerated the unraveling of Western imperial authority and opened the door to a rapid carousel of short‑lived emperors.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: An emperor’s fall on Roman ground paired with a reflective Italian mountainside journey creates a contrast between crumbling empires and timeless landscapes around the same peninsula.

queue.fact_text: In 1355, amid the Red Turban Rebellions, Han Lin’er—presented as a descendant of Song emperor Huizong—was proclaimed emperor of a ‘restored’ Song dynasty in Bozhou. Though largely a figurehead controlled by rebel leaders, his brief reign symbolized the power of lost dynasties to inspire new uprisings.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: Han Lin’er was effectively an imposed persona, an imperial ‘alter-ego’ crafted by rebels to rewrite history, aligning with an episode about constructed identities and parallel realities.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1916, U.S. General John J. Pershing led the 7th and 10th Cavalry regiments across the border into Mexico, launching the Punitive Expedition in pursuit of revolutionary leader Pancho Villa. It became a dusty, high‑stakes hunt across harsh terrain, testing logistics, politics, and the limits of cross‑border power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A bold incursion across a border matches the defiant, no‑permission energy of women in rock crashing through barriers of their own.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1925, a powerful earthquake near Dali in China’s Yunnan province, estimated around magnitude 7.0, devastated the region and killed approximately 5,000 people. Entire villages crumpled as traditional earthen and brick homes proved fatally vulnerable to the sudden shaking.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: An earthquake is nature’s brutal ‘electric shock’, suddenly releasing deep energy—this episode’s crackling guitars echo that violent jolt, translated into sound rather than destruction.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1978, Balkan Bulgarian Airlines Flight 107, a Tupolev Tu‑134, crashed near the village of Gabare, Bulgaria, killing all 73 people on board. The disaster, shrouded in Cold War–era secrecy, remains one of Bulgaria’s deadliest air accidents.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Both the crash and the orca dives turn the sky and sea into fragile, awe‑filled spaces where travel can shift suddenly from wonder to mortality, calling for a contemplative soundtrack.

queue.fact_text: Also on March 16, 1978, the supertanker Amoco Cadiz ran aground off Brittany’s Portsall Rocks and split in two, unleashing one of the largest oil spills in history at the time. Millions of gallons of crude blackened 300 kilometers of coastline, poisoning seabirds, shellfish beds, and entire marine ecosystems.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: The episode’s water‑like, flowing music contrasts poignantly with an ocean suffocated by oil, inviting listeners to imagine the sea both as sanctuary and as a victim of human error.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1962, Flying Tiger Line Flight 739 vanished over the western Pacific Ocean while transporting 93 U.S. soldiers and 11 crew, with no distress call and no wreckage ever conclusively found. Its disappearance remains one of aviation’s enduring mysteries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A slow, atmospheric set mirrors the eerie calm of a plane simply dissolving into the vastness of the Pacific, replacing anxiety with meditative reflection on the unknown.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1945, the ferocious Battle of Iwo Jima officially ended after more than a month of relentless fighting, though pockets of Japanese resistance held out in tunnels for weeks. The volcanic island became a symbol of sacrifice and a strategically vital stepping stone toward Japan.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: After the storm of Iwo Jima, the episode’s message that it ‘can’t rain forever’ offers a gentle postscript about endurance, healing, and the end of even the darkest campaigns.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1979, the short but bloody Sino‑Vietnamese War effectively ended when China’s People’s Liberation Army withdrew across the border back into China. The conflict had lasted just under a month, leaving tens of thousands dead while both sides claimed victory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A brief, border‑focused war ending with forces stepping back mirrors trip‑hop’s blurring and then re‑drawing of boundaries—tension, incursion, and then retreat into introspection.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1985, Associated Press journalist Terry Anderson was kidnapped in Beirut and held hostage for nearly seven years, becoming one of the longest‑held Western hostages in Lebanon’s civil war. His ordeal highlighted the risks of reporting from conflict zones and the human cost behind headline news.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Anderson’s long captivity demanded extreme persistence and psychological adaptation; the episode’s focus on slow progress, resilience, and the brain’s response to hardship offers a quiet parallel.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1988, U.S. Marine Lieutenant Colonel Oliver North and Vice Admiral John Poindexter were indicted for their roles in the Iran–Contra affair, a secret operation that funneled arms to Iran and money to Nicaraguan rebels. The scandal exposed a shadow foreign policy circumventing Congress.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A scandal of covert power and resistance to oversight pairs naturally with a politically charged episode centered on Rage Against the Machine’s critiques of state and corporate abuses.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 2014, Crimea held a controversial referendum in which officials claimed overwhelming support for seceding from Ukraine and joining Russia. Conducted under military occupation and widely rejected internationally, the vote dramatically redrew borders on the map but not in global consensus.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A disputed referendum about belonging and control mirrors an episode questioning who really controls our borders—physical or digital—when devices and networks shape our sense of home and autonomy.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 2012, Indian cricket legend Sachin Tendulkar became the first batter in history to score 100 international centuries, a milestone many thought impossible. His 100th hundred, made against Bangladesh, capped more than two decades of relentless, almost ritual consistency at the crease.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: Tendulkar’s 100th century represents a lifetime of momentum and forward motion; this upbeat ‘on the move’ mix mirrors the persistence, rhythm, and joy of an athlete who never stopped going.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1872, Wanderers F.C. defeated the Royal Engineers 1–0 at The Oval to win the very first FA Cup, the world’s oldest national football competition. Players wore heavy boots and shared positions fluidly, inventing the rhythms of modern football in real time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: That early FA Cup final turned a patch of grass into a ritual gathering place, much like the dancefloor is a field of movement and community—both are spaces where rhythm, bodies, and shared rules create magic.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 2002 isn’t the only musical milestone of this date—by a historical echo, the same day in 2012 saw Tendulkar’s 100th century, reminding us how March 16 often intertwines art and sport in unlikely symmetry. From concert halls to cricket grounds, it’s a date repeatedly chosen by history to stage endurance and performance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

queue.match_reason: Gubaidulina’s sacred composition and Tendulkar’s record both embody ‘timeless legacy’—works of discipline and feeling that continue to resonate far beyond the moment they were achieved.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 2022, a magnitude 7.4 earthquake struck off the coast of Fukushima, Japan, killing four people and injuring over 200. Occurring near the site of the 2011 megathrust quake and nuclear disaster, it was a stark reminder that the region’s tectonic story is still being written beneath the ocean floor.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: The quake’s aftershocks—physical and emotional—fit with an episode about weathering long storms and believing in eventual calm, echoing Japan’s enduring resilience in the face of repeated tremors.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 2014, the Crimean referendum’s results were used by Russia to annex the peninsula, altering lives along the Black Sea coast almost overnight. For many residents, borders shifted around them while their own identities and allegiances remained complex and contested.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: An episode about traveling through Eastern Europe when borders feel fragile mirrors Crimea’s abrupt redrawing of lines on the same regional map, with music tracing the human stories behind political shifts.

queue.fact_text: On March 16, 1966, while Gemini 8 was pioneering orbital docking, back on Earth engineers were already sketching concepts that would one day guide reusable spacecraft and space stations. The mission’s near‑disaster prompted major redesigns in attitude control systems, quietly improving the safety of most future flights.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: The mission’s trajectory from calm to chaos and back again mirrors the episode’s slow‑building ‘crescendo’—a journey from quiet control to expansive movement across celestial and earthly landscapes.

queue.fact_text: Long before rockets and tankers, March 16 has been a date when human choices reshaped coasts, borders, and skies—from the first FA Cup at The Oval to consitutions, coups, and cosmic experiments. Taken together, the events of this one day trace a curious pattern: our species keeps using the same calendar square to test how far we can push rules, frontiers, and even the planet itself.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A closing reflection on March 16 as a launchpad for big shifts pairs with Sedna FM’s own ‘where it all began’ episode—a reminder that every calendar square can be a starting point for something explosive and new.

published.fact_text: On March 16, 1926, Robert H. Goddard launched the world’s first liquid‑fueled rocket from a snowy farm in Auburn, Massachusetts. The spindly contraption flew for just 2.5 seconds and 56 meters—but it quietly opened the door to every modern space mission that followed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: Goddard’s tiny rocket turned empty sky into a new creative space for humanity, mirroring the episode’s theme of seeing emptiness as room to build something astonishing.

published.fact_text: In 1966, Gemini 8 carried Neil Armstrong and David Scott into orbit and achieved the first-ever docking of two spacecraft in space. The triumph nearly turned to disaster when a stuck thruster sent the docked vehicles into a wild spin, forcing Armstrong to perform a daring emergency undocking and reentry.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A perilous orbital rendezvous fits with a sound journey called “Destination Sedna,” both telling stories of bold navigation into unknown space.

published.fact_text: On March 16, 2002, in Hamburg’s Michaeliskirche, Sofia Gubaidulina’s monumental oratorio “Johannes-Ostern” received its premiere alongside her earlier “Johannes-Passion.” The pairing created an intense, mystical musical diptych that reimagined the Passion narrative with bold harmonies, unconventional instrumentation, and deep spiritual urgency.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: Gubaidulina’s sacred oratorios and flamenco share a raw, spiritual intensity and centuries of tradition being pushed into modern forms, making this episode a fitting musical echo.

published.fact_text: On March 16, 1898, delegates from five Australian colonies gathered in Melbourne to adopt a draft constitution that would become the foundation of the Commonwealth of Australia. It stitched together vast territories and diverse settlements into a single federated nation under a unique constitutional framework.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A new maritime-spanning federation born in Melbourne resonates with an episode sailing between coasts and cultures, both exploring how distant shores connect into one shared story.

published.fact_text: In 1815, Prince Willem of Orange proclaimed himself King of the United Kingdom of the Netherlands, becoming the country’s first constitutional monarch. His rule signaled a shift away from absolute power toward a modern system where royal authority was bound by a written constitution.
